summary: Gruber interviews Brent Simmons and Black Pixel's Daniel Pasco about the acquisition of NetNewsWire, the beloved RSS reader. Simmons explains how maintaining four separate versions across Mac and iOS became unsustainable for a single developer, despite his heroic efforts to build a unified codebase. Black Pixel, an iOS-focused development shop, plans to restore and extend NetNewsWire across all Apple platforms. The interview explores the technical debt of rushing to be in the App Store on day one, the challenge of cross-platform development even within Apple's ecosystem, and the emotional difficulty of letting go of a beloved creation. Gruber closes with a reflection on the intertwined history of NetNewsWire and Daring Fireball, both born in 2002 during the early days of RSS and Mac OS X.
key_points: Maintaining four app versions (Mac, Mac Lite, iPhone, iPad) with three separate codebases proved unsustainable for a single developer, even one as skilled as Simmons
Rushing to be in the App Store on day one for both iPhone and iPad was exciting but ultimately a strategic mistake that created years of technical debt
Cross-platform development between Mac and iOS is fundamentally hard because users don't care about shared code — they care about great platform-native UI
Black Pixel plans to honor NetNewsWire's design heritage while bringing team resources to realize its full potential, with the Mac version taking center stage
NetNewsWire and Daring Fireball have a symbiotic origin story — NNW drove early DF traffic through default feeds, and DF convinced Gruber that RSS mattered
money_quotes: Users don't actually care one tiny bit about shared code. Users want great software. They're right.
I didn't want to admit to myself that I'm not Superman. But I'm not. I'm good and always getting better. But my nerves are made of nerve tissue.
Safari's RSS support is about as effective for serious information consumption as the notes feature in Apple Mail is for GTD.
Everybody has thoughts not worthy of them. Me too.
The satisfaction I get from writing very good code is as important as any kudos I get from other people.

There may still be some developers who say there's no place for style and fashion in UI, that usability is usability and that's that. They're mistaken, because part of human nature is to get tired of things and want fresh things, and UI is where the computer meets human nature.
A good writer reads a lot of books. They see how other writers solve problems. I think good developers are the same: they look at other apps.
NetNewsWire single-handedly inspired me to add an RSS feed to Daring Fireball.
key_insight: Even within Apple's own ecosystem, building great software across Mac and iOS is so demanding that a single developer cannot sustain it — users care about platform-native excellence, not shared codebases.
